subject: Product Management
title: Windows Phone
description: A micro-lesson that makes Windows Phone usable.

hook.text: Windows Phone was praised for its beautiful design. Reviewers loved it. Users didn't buy it. Developers didn't build for it.

buildup.text: Microsoft's Live Tiles interface was genuinely innovative — dynamic, glanceable, and unlike anything from Apple or Google.

discovery.text: But apps made smartphones valuable. Developers built for iOS and Android first. Windows Phone had a barren app store.

twist.text: Microsoft paid developers to port apps. Without users, developers left. Without apps, users left. The chicken-and-egg problem won.

climax.text: Microsoft wrote off its $7.6 billion Nokia acquisition and exited smartphones. A better product lost to a better ecosystem.

punchline.text: A beautiful product with no ecosystem is a beautiful ghost town.

quiz.question: Why did Windows Phone fail despite good design?
options: The chicken-and-egg problem — no apps meant no users, and no users meant no apps
Microsoft priced Windows Phone too high
